nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical seagrass beds, sandy lagoons, reef flats, and rubble zones; burrows into the upper sand layer by day and emerges at night to graze.

decorPreferences: live rock with biofilm and algae

diet.notes: Generally accepted as an omnivorous scavenger that has been documented eating detritus, uneaten food, fish waste, diatoms, film algae, and cyanobacteria. If algae and detritus are scarce, supplemental feeding with sinking algae wafers or small bits of frozen food is commonly advised.

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x911. Live Cerithium nodulosum (Giant Knobby / Pacific Cerith, a listed synonym) in situ on sandy substrate, whole animal in frame with aperture visible; sharp, well-lit, natural seagrass/sand habitat matching the species profile.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x915. Live Cerithium nodulosum grazing on a reef flat, whole animal in frame; algae-encrusted shell typical of wild specimens. Good in-situ context shot.

summary: Cerith Snail is a small, peaceful sand-grazer prized as a saltwater cleanup crew member. Generally considered reef-safe, it spends its days burrowed in the upper sand layer and emerges at night to graze film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and detritus.
careNotes: Cerith Snail is an umbrella name for several closely related cerithiid sand-grazers commonly sold as cleanup crew, including Cerithium litteratum (Florida Cerith), Cerithium nodulosum (Pacific or Giant Knobby Cerith), and assorted Cerithium spp. At an adult size of roughly 2 to 3.5 cm, these snails spend the day burrowed in the upper few centimeters of sand and emerge after dark to graze film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, detritus, and uneaten food. They are commonly considered reef-safe and have not been reported to bother corals or other invertebrates. Like all molluscs, they are sensitive to copper-based medications, and they are also affected by elevated nitrate levels, so quarantine and slow drip acclimation are strongly recommended before adding them to a display tank. Cerith snails frequently lay distinctive rope-like egg strings on the aquarium glass after dark; the planktonic larvae rarely survive a normal display tank, as they are consumed by filter-feeders or removed by mechanical filtration, so the species does not typically overpopulate.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is generally considered impractical in a home aquarium. Mature snails frequently lay long, rope-like egg masses on the aquarium glass after dark, but the planktonic larvae require specialised rearing conditions and are typically consumed by tankmates or removed from the water column by filtration before they can settle and develop. Commercially maricultured specimens, including snails raised in Marshall Islands facilities, are available through specialist retailers, but home-tank propagation has rarely been reported.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn - Cerith Snails in the Reef Tank
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Cerithium litteratum, Caribbean native range, reef-safe status, omnivorous detritivore diet, nocturnal behavior, and ~1 snail per 5 gal stocking heuristic.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder - Cerith Snail Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Third source confirmed adult size 2-3 cm, temperature 22-27 C, pH 8.0-8.5, salinity SG 1.023-1.025, kH 7-16 dKH, lifespan up to 3 years, and deep-sand-bed burrowing behavior.
